method_atom: a numerical study of many coupled continuous angular (phase) variables with pairwise alignment interactions along the edges of an underlying network, asking whether a symmetry-breaking order-disorder transition exists and of what order, as the network's geometry is varied (short-path/small-world connectivity versus a negatively curved tiling versus a flat regular grid). The presence and character of the transition are diagnosed by finite-size scaling of a fourth-order fluctuation ratio (Binder cumulant), by the density of topological defects, and by long-wavelength fluctuation modes obtained from a low-'noise' correlation-function expansion, showing how connectivity structure and long-wavelength fluctuations control whether long-range order survives.
object_atom: the XY model of planar (two-component) spins on a heptagonal lattice with negative curvature, compared against a flat two-dimensional square lattice and a small-world network, with vortices as the topological defects and spinwaves as the fluctuation modes.
brief_reason: The transferable part is the machinery of pairwise-aligned continuous phase variables on a network and the toolkit (Binder-cumulant scaling, defect density, long-wavelength fluctuation analysis) for deciding whether curvature/connectivity permits an ordering transition; the domain-specific part is the physics of XY spins, vortices, and spinwaves on the specific curved/flat/small-world geometries.
